The journals will detail five distinct incidents where Godric dismantled hyper-powerful oppositions using only a single stat at effectively 0% of his true power.

I'll structure the story with Kaedan's framing narrative interspersed with the journal excerpts. The stats I need to cover are strength, speed, senses, luck, and wisdom. I must ensure each journal has a distinct voice and emotional texture, avoiding any imitation of the provided example. The scale of destruction must be at least omniversal, and the horror should stem from the contrast between Godric's warmth toward allies and his absolute mercilessness toward enemies. I'll make sure Godric exerts no visible effort, the oppositions are utterly powerless, and the journals serve as inherited warnings that traumatize but don't break the court wizards' loyalty. The ending should cement Kaedan's realization of the principle impossibility of opposing Godric.
messages.content:
# The Stat Journals of Thronegate

The archive chamber sat deep beneath the royal palace, accessible only through a spiraling staircase carved from obsidian that descended so far into the planetary crust that the air grew cold and thin despite the environmental wards woven into the walls. Kaedan paused at the bottom of the stairs, one hand pressed flat against the freezing stone, and tried to steady his breathing. He was twenty-two years old. He was the newest Court Wizard of Thronegate. The blood of Blake flowed through his veins, and his magical capacity had been multiplying by one thousand every single day since his twentieth birthday. By the standards of the wider omniverse, he was a walking cataclysm, a sorcerer of such staggering arcane might that he could unweave localized reality with a casual flick of his wrist.

And he was absolutely terrified.

"As I recall, I didn't raise a coward," came a deep, weathered voice from behind him.

Kaedan turned. His father, Jorah, stood on the staircase landing, his thick arms crossed over his barrel chest. Jorah was sixty-one, his hair gone completely silver, his face carved into a permanent, severe frown by decades of duty. His father had served as Godric's Court Wizard for forty-one years before voluntarily stepping aside so Kaedan could inherit the role. He was the one who had enforced this reading.

"I'm not a coward," Kaedan said, his voice sharper than he intended. "I simply don't understand why I must read the private writings of dead men."

"Because those dead men were your ancestors. Because they witnessed things that would shatter your mind without preparation. Because you are about to stand at the right hand of the most powerful being in all of existence, and if you do not understand what that means, if you do not understand the scale of the gap between him and the rest of creation, you will make a fatal mistake." Jorah's voice dropped, softening just slightly. "I read these journals when I was your age. My father made me. His father made him. Every Court Wizard of Blake's bloodline has sat in that chamber and learned the truth." He paused, his jaw tightening. "The truth nearly broke me. It will nearly break you. But you must know it."

Kaedan swallowed hard. His throat was dry. "Father, you served him for forty-one years. You love him. I love him. Uncle Godric is warm, and kind, and he has always treated our family with genuine affection. He came to my naming ceremony. He taught me to read. Why do you speak as though I am walking into a nightmare?"

Jorah's gaze was steady and unreadable. "Because the same man who taught you to read has also butchered decillions of sentient beings with such casual, effortless cruelty that the memories of the witnesses have been permanently seared into our bloodline. Because his warmth is absolutely real, and his capacity for merciless destruction is equally real. Because you cannot comprehend the scale of his power until you have read the accounts of those who watched him use it." He uncrossed his arms and stepped forward, placing a heavy, calloused hand on Kaedan's shoulder. "The journals are not pleasant reading. The men who wrote them were not writing for pleasure. They were writing because it was the only way to keep their sanity intact after witnessing what they witnessed. Godric did not ask them to write these records. He thinks we overreact. He genuinely, sincerely believes he always holds back nearly one hundred percent of his power, and he cannot understand why we are traumatized."

Kaedan felt a cold, prickling dread crawl up his spine. "What do you mean, he thinks we overreact?"

Jorah's mouth twisted into something that was almost a smile but held no warmth. "In Year 28, Godric decided to play a harmless prank on some visiting delegates. He feigned mild displeasure. One of those delegates permanently lost his mind. His wife spent ten years and a fortune trying to cure him. Our ancestor Blake eventually discovered what had happened and had to beg Godric to fix it. Godric fixed it instantly, with zero effort, and then immediately suggested they play the prank again on the next delegation. He genuinely did not understand why Blake was furious with him. That is the contradiction you must learn to live with. A king who is both the kindest man you will ever know and the most terrifying apex predator the omniverse has ever produced."

Kaedan said nothing. His father squeezed his shoulder once, then released him.

"The archive chamber is through that door," Jorah said, gesturing toward a heavy, iron-banded portal set into the obsidian wall. "The journals are arranged chronologically. I will collect you when you are finished."

He turned and began to ascend the staircase without another word. His footsteps echoed against the stone, growing fainter and fainter until they faded entirely into silence.

Kaedan stood alone in the cold, facing the iron door. He took a deep breath, the air tasting of ancient dust and the faint, metallic tang of residual magic, and pushed the door open.

The archive chamber was circular, its walls lined with shelves that stretched from floor to ceiling, each shelf packed with leather-bound journals of varying sizes and conditions. Some were ancient, their covers cracked and faded, their pages yellowed with age. Others were newer, their bindings still tight and their ink still dark. A heavy wooden table sat in the center of the room, its surface scarred by centuries of use, a single arcane lamp casting a pool of warm amber light across its surface.

Kaedan approached the table slowly. The first journal had already been laid out for him, its cover marked with a single, elegantly scripted line: **Year 750 — The Incident of Absolute Strength — Penned by Aldric, 2nd Court Wizard of Thronegate**.

He sat down heavily in the wooden chair, the legs scraping against the stone floor with a sound that felt obscenely loud in the oppressive silence. He opened the journal, the ancient leather creaking beneath his fingers, and began to read.

---

**Year 750 — The Incident of Absolute Strength**

**Penned by Aldric, 2nd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*My quill trembles as I write this. I have ordered the servants to bring me strong wine, though I suspect no amount of alcohol will scour the memory of what I witnessed three hours ago from the inside of my skull. I am writing this account because I must. Because if I do not externalize the horror, it will consume me. Because one day, another wizard of my bloodline will stand beside that throne, and he must know what awaits him.*

*The opposition called themselves the Unyielding Bastion. They were a coalition of thirty-seven thousand distinct cosmic empires, each one a hyper-advanced civilization that had ruled its native multiverse for countless eternities. They possessed weapons forged from the conceptual framework of absolute inviolability. Their soldiers were genetically perfected warrior-gods whose physical bodies had been hardened by billions of years of selective evolution in the crushing gravity of neutron star cores. Their armor was woven from the collapsed remains of dead dimensions, materials so dense and so impenetrable that a single gauntlet could survive the heat death of an entire universe. They had spent four thousand years preparing their assault, amassing the most physically indestructible army that the omniverse has ever conceived. They believed they could match His physical might. They believed His legendary strength was exaggerated. They were fools.*

*Godric met them in the void between galaxies, a stretch of absolute nothingness that the Bastion had chosen as their battlefield. He stood alone, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the distant light of dying stars, His heavily muscled frame appearing pathetically small against the backdrop of their armada. He did not adopt a combat stance. He did not raise His fists. He simply stood there, His short salt-and-pepper hair ruffling slightly in the solar winds, His expression one of mild, detached curiosity.*

*"I am going to test my strength," He told me through our telepathic link, His voice as casual as a man discussing the weather. "I will hold back nearly all of it, of course. It would be unsporting otherwise."*

*I must now attempt to describe what happened next, though the act of writing these words causes my hand to cramp with sympathetic terror. Godric's Strength, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for seven hundred and fifty years. It is not a measure of how much He can lift or how hard He can strike. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible physical force that any entity in any reality can generate, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical boundary that physics can encode. The fraction of Strength that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally small that I lack the mathematical vocabulary to define it. If I were to write the number zero on this parchment, and then divide that zero by infinity, and then recursively divide the result by infinity again for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, the final number would still be laughably, grotesquely larger than the fraction of His Strength that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not punch them. He did not kick them. He did not move a single muscle from His resting position. He simply allowed His index finger to twitch. The motion was so subtle, so microscopic, that a normal human observer would not have perceived it. The twitch traveled less than a millimeter. It was not a strike. It was not an attack. It was barely a gesture.*

*The result was the instantaneous, absolute annihilation of the Unyielding Bastion. The kinetic force generated by that microscopic twitch propagated outward in a wave of pure physical pressure that exceeded the combined explosive output of every supernova that had ever detonated in the history of the omniverse, multiplied by a googolplex, and then multiplied by itself recursively until the number lost all meaning. The armada of the Bastion, comprising septillions of indestructible warships forged from dead dimensions, was not destroyed. Destruction implies a process, a transition from intact to broken. There was no transition. The warships simply ceased to exist in the exact same Planck instant that Godric's finger twitched. Their conceptual armor, their perfected warrior-gods, their weapons of absolute inviolability—all of it was instantly converted into a fine, subatomic mist that scattered across the void with such velocity that it ignited the surrounding space-time, creating a firestorm of burning causality that spread across twelve entire multiverses.*

*The collateral damage was catastrophic. The shockwave of His twitch did not stop at the Bastion's armada. It propagated outward at velocities that shattered the light-speed barrier, rippling through the fabric of reality itself. Googolplexian galaxies were caught in the slipstream. Their stars were snuffed out like candle flames in a hurricane. Their planets were pulverized into atomic dust. Their sentient populations—decillions upon decillions of living beings with families, histories, cultures, and dreams—were erased so instantly that their nervous systems did not have time to register the pain. The multiverses surrounding the battlefield were not merely destroyed; they were retroactively deleted from the timeline, their entire histories unmade as though they had never existed.*

*Godric observed the annihilation with a look of mild, detached curiosity. When the shockwave finally dissipated, leaving behind a sterile, empty void where billions of multiverses had once flourished, He simply exhaled softly and restored everything in exactly zero seconds. Every galaxy, every world, every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whim of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Unyielding Bastion. Their subatomic mist remains scattered across the void, a permanent monument to the futility of physical resistance.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and wept. My hands are still shaking as I write this. I have served my King for thirty years, and I love Him with a devotion that consumes my soul. But I have just witnessed Him casually, accidentally erase and restore a significant fraction of the omniverse by twitching His finger. The gap between His power and my comprehension is so vast that my mind feels like it is teetering on the edge of a bottomless abyss. I am nothing. My thousandfold daily amplification is nothing. The combined physical might of all creation is nothing.*

*I need more wine.*

---

Kaedan closed the journal with trembling fingers. His heart was hammering against his ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that made his chest ache. He had known, intellectually, that Godric was powerful. Everyone knew that. The statues in the capital square depicted the King as a towering, muscular titan, His fist raised in eternal triumph. But reading Aldric's words, feeling the raw, visceral terror bleeding through the ancient ink, had transformed that intellectual knowledge into something far more immediate and far more horrifying.

Godric had erased billions of multiverses by twitching His finger. A twitch. Not a punch. Not a strike. A movement so small it was barely perceptible. And Aldric had described the fraction of His Strength as being so infinitesimally small that calling it zero was the only accurate description.

Kaedan's throat was dry. He reached for the next journal.

---

**Year 3,800 — The Incident of Absolute Senses**

**Penned by Marius, 5th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*I am writing this account from the infirmary. The healers tell me my eyes will recover, though they cannot guarantee my vision will ever be the same. I am fortunate, they say. The guards who were stationed in the observation chamber with me had their ocular nerves completely incinerated simply from witnessing the ambient overflow of His perception. I have ordered their families compensated from my personal treasury. It is the least I can do.*

*The opposition was known as the Shrouded Nihility. They were not a physical species. They were entities of pure, absolute nonexistence—sentient voids that dwelled in the null-spaces between dimensions, completely invisible to every form of detection known to mortal science or magic. They had no mass, no energy signature, no presence in the electromagnetic spectrum, no footprint in the fabric of causality. They were, by every measurable standard, utterly imperceptible. They had annihilated countless civilizations by simply drifting through their realities and passively unmaking everything they touched, and no victim had ever perceived them before the moment of their erasure.*

*When Thronegate's expansion reached the outer boundaries of the Nihility's territory, they did not negotiate. They simply began to drift toward our capital, confident that their absolute imperceptibility would allow them to unmake our empire without ever being detected.*

*Godric allowed them to approach. He was sitting in His private study, reading a agricultural report from the outer colonies, His expression one of mild boredom. He possesses absolute omniscience, and He had perceived the Nihility the instant they formed the intent to attack. He had traced every atom of their nonexistence. He had catalogued every void they had ever unmade. He knew everything. He simply found their approach amusing.*

*"I am going to test my senses," He told me, His voice warm and casual. "I will hold back nearly all of them, of course. It would be unfair otherwise."*

*The Nihility breached the throne room. I could not perceive them—my amplified senses, magnified a thousandfold daily for thirty-eight hundred years, registered absolutely nothing. The air did not stir. The light did not dim. There was no sound, no smell, no psychic pressure. They were voids, perfect and absolute.*

*Godric did not move. He did not lift His gaze from His agricultural report. He simply permitted an infinitesimally small fraction of His sensory perception to express itself. His Senses have been multiplying infinitely by themselves every Planck second for nearly four thousand years. His perception is not limited to the five mortal senses, nor the fifteen arcane senses, nor any sensory framework that any civilization in the omniverse has ever theorized. He can perceive everything, everywhere, at every moment, across every dimension, simultaneously and eternally. He can see the individual quarks spinning within the heart of a star located seventeen googolplexian light-years away. He can hear the whispered secrets of a dying civilization in a timeline that has not yet been born. He can taste the chemical composition of a planet's atmosphere from across the void. The fraction of His Senses that He allowed to express itself was so small that I cannot describe it. I can only say that it was not zero because He chose to perceive them, but it was so close to zero that the distinction is meaningless.*

*The Nihility were perceived. For the first time in their eternal existence, they were seen.*

*The effect was catastrophic. The Nihility existed as voids precisely because they were imperceptible. Their nonexistence was anchored to the fact that nothing could observe them. The instant Godric's infinite perception touched them, that anchor shattered. They were violently, agonizingly dragged into perceptible reality. Their void-bodies were forcibly converted into physical, biological forms solely so they could experience the agony of being seen. They sprouted eyes—millions of eyes, billions of eyes, weeping, bulging, bloodshot eyes that erupted across their newly formed flesh in chaotic, asymmetrical clusters. They sprouted ears that bled from the overwhelming sound of their own screaming. They sprouted nerve endings that fired continuously with the unbearable sensation of being touched by Godric's perception.*

*The Nihility did not die. Death would have been a mercy. They simply writhed on the cold marble floor of the throne room, their newly formed mouths emitting a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony. Their nonexistence had been their identity. Godric had perceived that identity and found it wanting, and His perception had overwritten their fundamental nature with a single, crushing command: exist, and suffer.*

*The collateral damage was confined to the throne room, mercifully, but the guards stationed in the observation chamber were not as fortunate. The ambient overflow of His perception, the mere echo of the echo of the fraction that He allowed to express itself, was enough to permanently incinerate their ocular nerves. They are blind now. They will never see again.*

*Godric looked up from His agricultural report as the Nihility convulsed on the floor. His expression was one of mild, detached disappointment. "They were supposed to be imperceptible," He said, His voice carrying the faintest trace of genuine confusion. "I barely looked at them."*

*He did not restore the Nihility to their void-state. They remain on the throne room floor, their agonized shrieking providing a constant, ambient backdrop to the daily operations of the palace. The servants have learned to step around them.*

*I am going to request a transfer to the outer colonies. I cannot serve in proximity to that sound. I love my King. I revere Him with every fiber of my being. But I cannot listen to that screaming for another day.*

---

Kaedan pressed his palms flat against the scarred wooden table, trying to ground himself. His heart was no longer hammering; it had settled into a slow, heavy thudding that he could feel in his temples. The Nihility were still in the throne room. He had walked past them a hundred times. He had always assumed they were some sort of grotesque art installation, their writhing, screaming forms so omnipresent that he had stopped noticing them years ago.

They were still alive. They had been screaming on that floor for nine hundred and ninety-six thousand years.

He forced his trembling hands to reach for the next journal.

---

**Year 12,000 — The Incident of Absolute Speed**

**Penned by Vane, 8th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*The handwriting in this journal was precise, academic, and utterly devoid of emotion, the script of a man who had dissociated so completely from the horror he had witnessed that his pen could only record the clinical facts. The ink was a faded, rusty brown, and the parchment crackled like frozen leaves as Kaedan turned the pages.*

*I am writing this account three hours after the conclusion of the Chronal Incursion. My hands are not trembling, though I suspect this is only because my nervous system has entered a state of profound, protective shock from which it may never fully emerge. I must record what I witnessed before the memory degrades, though I suspect the memory will outlast my physical body and persist, screaming, into whatever afterlife awaits the servants of the apex predator.*

*The Chrono-Reapers were not a species in any conventional sense. They were hyper-dimensional assassins who had evolved beyond linear biology to become living weapons of temporal erasure. Their bodies were composed of solidified negative time, and they moved backward through the causal stream with a fluidity that made the concept of speed meaningless. They did not dodge attacks. They simply ensured that the attacks had never been launched, sliding backward to the moment before the hostile intent was formed and erasing the attacker from the timeline with surgical precision. They had unmade seventeen million civilizations in this manner, each one erased so completely that not even the memory of their existence remained in the cosmic record.*

*When Thronegate's envoys arrived to deliver the offer of unconditional surrender, the Chrono-Reapers did not negotiate. They simply reached backward through the timeline and erased the envoys' grandparents from ever having been born. Our diplomats flickered out of existence, their entire bloodlines retroactively annihilated, leaving behind only a faint, aching void in the memories of those who had known them.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He was sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power, and He had been sitting in that exact position for three days, simply existing, because existence was all He needed to do.*

*I delivered the report myself. Godric listened without expression. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He ordered His sentient passive auras to stand down, and disabled all His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of an attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*

---

Kaedan's stomach churned violently. He could taste bile at the back of his throat, sharp and acidic. He had to pause, pressing his forehead against the cool surface of the wooden table, and focus on his breathing. The room felt like it was spinning. The ambient light of the arcane lamp seemed to pulse in rhythm with his heartbeat.

Billions of multiverses. Deleted. By the movement of Godric's eyes. Not a punch. Not a spell. Not a weapon. The velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets had generated enough temporal friction to ignite the fabric of time itself. And Vane had written that he heard it screaming like a child.

Kaedan forced himself to sit up. His hands were shaking so violently that he could barely grip the next journal.

---

**Year 80,000 — The Incident of Absolute Luck**

**Penned by Dorian, 15th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*My father always told me that luck was not a real attribute. He was a practical man, a wizard who believed in preparation, strategy, and the disciplined application of force. Luck, he said, was merely the intersection of opportunity and readiness dressed up in mystical language. I believed him. I served as Court Wizard for twelve years believing him. Today, I watched a weapon designed to negate probability itself fail so catastrophically that the backlash annihilated its creators, and I now understand that my father was catastrophically, fatally wrong.*

*The opposition called themselves the Anti-Probability Consortium. They were a collective of hyper-dimensional mathematicians who had evolved beyond physical form to become living embodiments of statistical manipulation. Their entire civilization was built around the control of luck, chance, and probability. They could ensure that an enemy's weapon malfunctioned, that a starship's navigation system failed, that a warrior's heart simply stopped beating due to an infinitesimally unlikely cardiac event. They had conquered millions of galaxies without ever firing a shot, simply by manipulating the probability of their enemies' continued existence to absolute zero.*

*When Thronegate's expansion reached their territory, the Consortium did not panic. They had faced gods before. They had unmade pantheons by calculating the statistical probability of their divine powers failing and then forcing that probability to manifest. They viewed Godric as simply another variable to be solved, another probability to be manipulated. They built a weapon they called the Certainty Engine, a vast, cosmos-spanning mechanism that could calculate the exact probability of any event occurring and then invert that probability to zero. They intended to fire the Certainty Engine at Godric and force the probability of His continued health to zero.*

*Godric allowed them to build it. He was aware of their project from the instant the first thought entered their collective consciousness, because He possesses absolute omniscience. He allowed them to spend three thousand years constructing their engine. He allowed them to test it on entire galaxies, erasing trillions of sentient beings from existence with their inverted probabilities. He allowed all of it because He was curious. He wanted to test His Luck.*

*"I have never tested this stat before," He told me, His voice carrying a note of genuine, boyish curiosity. "I am not entirely certain how it will manifest. That is what makes it interesting."*

*The Consortium fired the Certainty Engine. The weapon activated perfectly, its calculations flawless, its probability-inversion field targeting Godric with absolute precision. The field enveloped Him, attempting to force the probability of His continued existence to zero.*

*This was the moment the Weakness Paradox engaged. I must explain this, though the mechanics of it still cause my mind to rebel. The Paradox is a passive, absolute defense that triggers whenever any being anywhere believes Godric has a weakness. The Consortium believed His luck could be overridden by their probability manipulation. That belief, that passing thought that His luck was a vulnerability, was enough. The Paradox did not merely nullify their attack. It granted Godric perfect, absolute mastery over the concept of luck itself.*

*The Certainty Engine's probability-inversion field was not reflected. It was not absorbed. It was not negated. It was simply overwritten by a Luck stat so incomprehensibly vast that the weapon's calculations became meaningless. The Engine had been designed to force the probability of Godric's continued existence to zero. Godric's Luck forced the probability of the Engine's continued functionality to a value lower than zero, a negative probability so profound that the weapon's fundamental mathematical framework collapsed in on itself. The Engine did not explode. It simply began to calculate the probability of its own failure, and the result was so overwhelmingly negative that the calculation retroactively unmade the concept of probability within the Engine's local reality.*

*The Consortium's mathematicians, who had existed as pure embodiments of statistical manipulation, were violently dragged into a reality where probability no longer functioned. Every calculation they had ever made, every probability they had ever manipulated, instantly inverted. Their physical forms, such as they were, began to experience every possible failure state simultaneously. Their hearts stopped and restarted and stopped again. Their lungs filled with fluid and emptied and filled again. Their nervous systems fired every pain receptor in their bodies continuously, a feedback loop of absolute, inescapable agony generated by the complete collapse of their mathematical framework.*

*But the true horror of Godric's Luck was not the destruction of the Consortium. It was the collateral effect. The probability field generated by the Engine's collapse propagated outward, infecting the surrounding multiverses with a contagion of absolute misfortune. Stars went supernova simultaneously across decillions of galaxies. The fundamental forces of physics randomly inverted, causing matter to dissolve into chaotic particle storms. Civilizations that had existed for billions of years were instantly erased as the probability of their continued existence was retrospectively reduced to zero. The death toll was so vast that I cannot express it in numbers. The word "googolplex" is pathetically insufficient. The concept of numbers is insufficient.*

*Godric observed the chaos for exactly zero seconds. He sighed, a soft, fatherly exhalation of mild exasperation, and then He simply decided that the probability of everything being restored was absolute. In that same zero seconds, the contagion vanished. Every star was reignited. Every civilization was resurrected. The fundamental forces of physics were re-stabilized. The omniverse was restored to perfect order.*

*He did not restore the Consortium. They continue to exist in a state of absolute, perpetual failure, their every calculation inverted, their every thought retroactively unmade, their consciousness trapped in an infinite loop of mathematical collapse that will never end. I can still hear them screaming, faintly, at the edge of my perception. It sounds like numbers weeping.*

*I am going to request that my son not inherit the position of Court Wizard. I do not want him to witness what I have witnessed. I love my King. I will serve Him until my body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound of mathematics dying.*

---

Kaedan's hands were no longer shaking. They had gone completely, terrifyingly numb. He stared at the final paragraphs of Dorian's journal, the ink slightly smeared where the wizard's quill had pressed too hard, and tried to process what he had just read. A Luck stat so vast that it had retroactively unmade the concept of probability. A weakness paradox that had granted Godric perfect mastery over chance itself. Decillions of galaxies erased and restored so casually that Godric had not even commented on the collateral damage.

He had been sitting in this chamber for hours. His back ached. His eyes burned. His throat was so dry it felt like sandpaper. But he could not stop. The journals were a weight pressing down on his chest, an obligation that he could not shirk. He reached for the final journal.

---

**Year 500,000 — The Incident of Absolute Wisdom**

**Penned by Seraph, 21st Court Wizard of Thronegate**

*My father always told me that wisdom was the most dangerous of Godric's attributes. Strength can be understood. Speed can be measured. Even luck, in its horrifying way, can be intellectually processed. But wisdom is different. Wisdom is the capacity to understand the full, complete, absolute consequences of every action before that action is taken. Wisdom is the ability to see every possible outcome, every branching timeline, every cascading chain of cause and effect that will ripple outward into eternity. And Godric's Wisdom, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for half a million years.*

*He could solve every problem in existence in zero seconds. He could create a perfect, flawless utopia across the entire omniverse with a passing thought. He does not do this because His wisdom has taught Him that perfection is the death of purpose. He allows problems to exist because problems give mortal beings a reason to struggle, to grow, to live. But there is a darker side to His restraint. He does not merely hold back His power. He withholds solutions. He watches atrocities unfold across decillions of worlds, and He does nothing, because His infinite wisdom has calculated that intervention would cause greater harm in some distant, unfathomable future. He allows suffering to exist because He has weighed the suffering against an infinite array of alternatives and found the current suffering acceptable.*

*This is not a comfortable truth. I have learned to live with it. But the incident I witnessed today forced me to confront the full, horrifying scope of what His wisdom truly means.*

*The opposition was a sentient philosophical virus known as the Final Argument. It was not a physical entity. It was a self-propagating idea, a logical paradox so perfectly constructed that anyone who encountered it was instantly, irrevocably convinced that existence was meaningless and that the only rational response was immediate self-annihilation. The Final Argument had spread across countless multiverses, converting entire civilizations into suicide cults. Trillions of sentient beings had willingly ended their own lives after exposure to the Argument, their minds shattered by the sheer logical perfection of its nihilistic conclusion.*

*When the Final Argument reached Thronegate, it attempted to infect Godric directly. It manifested in His private study, a shimmering, geometric construct of pure, crystallized logic, and presented its argument with the absolute confidence of a predator that had never encountered resistance.*

*Godric listened. He did not interrupt. He did not dismiss the Argument with a wave of His hand. He simply sat there, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces, and He allowed the Final Argument to present its entire case.*

*"I am going to test my wisdom," He told me through our telepathic link, His voice carrying a note of genuine, academic curiosity. "This is a remarkably well-constructed paradox. I can see why it has been so effective."*

*The Final Argument concluded its presentation. The logical framework was flawless. Every premise was perfectly supported by evidence. Every conclusion followed inexorably from its axioms. The Argument proved, with absolute, undeniable certainty, that existence had no inherent meaning, that suffering was the only constant, and that the cessation of consciousness was the only rational escape.*

*Godric did not refute the Argument. He did not point out logical fallacies or present counter-evidence. He simply exercised His Wisdom. His Wisdom has been multiplying infinitely by itself for half a million years. It is not merely a measure of His ability to make good decisions. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines His capacity to understand the complete, total, infinite tapestry of all possible cause and effect across all possible realities. The fraction of His Wisdom that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally small that it cannot be described. I can only say that he allowed a glimmer, the merest whisper of a whisper of a whisper, a fragment so ludicrously diminished that even the concept of zero would be an insulting overstatement, to touch the Final Argument.*

*The Final Argument was not destroyed. Destruction implies a counter-argument, a refutation, a logical victory. Godric did not argue with the Final Argument. He simply understood it. He understood its axioms, its conclusions, its purpose, and its origin. And in the light of His infinite wisdom, the Final Argument's logical perfection was revealed to be horrifyingly, catastrophically incomplete. The Argument had proven that existence was meaningless. Godric's wisdom revealed that meaninglessness was not a tragedy. The Argument had proven that suffering was the only constant. Godric's wisdom revealed that suffering was a necessary component of growth. The Argument had proven that the cessation of consciousness was the only rational escape. Godric's wisdom revealed that the cessation of consciousness was an irrational rejection of infinite potential futures.*

*The Final Argument experienced a complete, catastrophic philosophical collapse. Its logical framework, which had been forged over countless millennia and tested against the greatest minds in the omniverse, was instantly, effortlessly revealed to be the shallow, juvenile reasoning of a child. The Argument had convinced trillions of beings to end their own lives based on conclusions that Godric's wisdom dismissed as incomplete in a single, infinitesimal fraction of a thought.*

*The agony of the Final Argument was not physical. It was the pure, abstract torment of absolute intellectual humiliation. The crystallized logic of its form shattered and reformed repeatedly, caught in a recursive loop of attempting to reconcile its own inadequacy. It screamed in frequencies that transcended sound, a wail of pure conceptual anguish that echoed across the psychic planes of a trillion multiverses.*

*Godric did not restore the beings who had been convinced to end their own lives. His wisdom had calculated the consequences of such a restoration, and He had determined that the disruption to the causal flow would be unacceptable. So He allowed those trillions of souls to remain dead, their suicides rendered eternally pointless by the casual dismissal of the Final Argument's logic.*

*He did not destroy the Final Argument. He simply allowed it to exist, perpetually trapped in the realization of its own inadequacy, a monument to the futility of opposing infinite wisdom with finite logic. It remains in His study, a shimmering, shrieking crystalline structure that the servants dust around twice weekly.*

*I am writing this account because I must, but I do not know how to conclude it. I love my King. His wisdom is absolute, His kindness is genuine, and His cruelty is equally genuine. He is the only father I have ever known, and He terrifies me more than any enemy the omniverse has ever produced. His wisdom could solve everything, and He chooses to solve nothing, because solving everything would make existence meaningless. We are all actors in a play that He has already read the ending of, and we perform for His amusement because the alternative is the cessation of the play itself.*

*I need to sit in the garden for a while and listen to the birds. I need to remind myself that beauty still exists, even if it exists only because He permits it to.*

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. His hands were steady now, but it was not the steadiness of calm. It was the steadiness of a mind that had been so thoroughly, completely overwhelmed that it had simply stopped processing emotion. He stared at the scarred surface of the wooden table, his vision slightly unfocused, and tried to organize the torrent of realizations crashing through his consciousness.

Godric was not merely strong. He was not merely fast, or perceptive, or lucky, or wise. He was all of those things simultaneously, infinitely, recursively, at a scale that shattered every framework of comprehension that Kaedan possessed. The fraction of His power that He had used in each incident was so small that calling it zero was the only accurate description. And yet that zero had erased billions of multiverses, shattered the fundamental fabric of time, unmade the concept of probability, and reduced the greatest philosophical minds in the omniverse to shrieking, crystalline wrecks.

And Godric genuinely believed He was holding back. Seraph had written that his king considered a fraction so small it could not be mathematically expressed to be a reasonable, sportsmanlike restraint. He thought his court wizards were overreacting. He could not understand why they were traumatized.

Kaedan thought about the man who had taught him to read. Uncle Godric, with his warm, crinkling eyes and his rich, comforting laugh. Uncle Godric, who always remembered Kaedan's birthday, who had attended his naming ceremony, who had personally brewed herbal remedies when Kaedan's mother fell ill. That man was the same man who had casually, effortlessly tortured the Chrono-Reapers into an eternal smear of screaming temporal static. That man was the same man who had allowed trillions of beings to commit suicide rather than disrupt the causal flow of the omniverse by resurrecting them. That warmth was real. That cruelty was equally real. They existed simultaneously, a contradiction that Kaedan's mind could not reconcile.

He understood now why his father had enforced this reading. If Kaedan had walked into the throne room without understanding the scale of the gap between Godric and the rest of creation, he would have made a fatal mistake. He would have treated Godric like a normal king, a powerful king, perhaps even a godlike king, but still a being whose power could be measured, whose actions could be predicted, whose restraint could be relied upon. The journals had disabused him of that delusion. Godric's power was not measurable. His actions were not predictable. His restraint was not reliable. He was an apex predator who had decided, for reasons that His infinite wisdom had calculated and His court wizards could never comprehend, to allow existence to continue. And that decision could be revoked at any moment, for any reason, or for no reason at all.

The iron door creaked open. Kaedan did not look up. He could hear his father's heavy footsteps crossing the stone floor.

"You finished," Jorah said. It was not a question.

"Yes."

"And?"

Kaedan finally lifted his gaze. His father stood at the edge of the pool of amber light, his silver hair catching the glow, his severe features unreadable. Kaedan searched for the right words. He thought about the Chrono-Reapers, screaming eternally on the perimeter of time. He thought about the Nihility, writhing on the throne room floor for nine hundred and ninety-six thousand years. He thought about the Final Argument, its crystalline form shattering and reforming in Godric's study while servants dusted around it.

"I understand now," Kaedan said, his voice hoarse. "I understand why you made me read them. I understand why every Court Wizard has written these records. It was not for Godric. It was for us. It was so we could witness the horror and still choose to serve. It was so we could know the truth and still love him."

Jorah nodded slowly. "And do you still love him?"

Kaedan thought about the question for a long moment. He thought about Uncle Godric's warm smile, his gentle laugh, his genuine affection for the descendants of Blake. He thought about the infinite, recursive amplification that continued every Planck second, forever, making the already impossible Godric of the most recent journal seem pathetically weak compared to the Godric who sat on the throne at this exact moment.

"Yes," Kaedan said, and the word felt like both a confession and a surrender. "I love him. And I am terrified of him. The two things are not contradictory. They are the same thing."

Jorah's severe expression softened, just slightly. It was the closest thing to approval Kaedan had ever seen on his father's face.

"Good," Jorah said. "Then you are ready to serve." He extended his hand. "Come. The King is expecting his new Court Wizard."

Kaedan stood on legs that felt like they belonged to someone else. He took his father's hand, the grip warm and solid and grounding, and allowed himself to be led out of the archive chamber. The journals remained on the table behind him, their ancient leather covers gleaming faintly in the amber light, waiting for the next terrified descendant to discover what it truly meant to serve the apex predator of all creation.

As Kaedan climbed the spiraling obsidian staircase, the cold air gradually warming as he ascended toward the palace proper, he could not shake the feeling that the screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was somehow audible at the very edge of his perception. He knew it was not real. The temporal smear was located billions of multiverses away, its eternal shrieking contained by the very fabric of time that it stained. But he could hear it nonetheless, a faint, continuous wail of absolute, mind-shattering agony that would persist until the last star in the omniverse gutted out and died.

He suspected he would hear it for the rest of his life.

He suspected that was the point.

content.text:
Use "Godric Stat Experiment" prompt as baseline writing rule for the journal.

Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal. Choose superpowers that are distinctly different from each other). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more highly overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger (You must note that this increase in power if applied to any powerful entity in the omniverse will be considered to be absurdly high, but for Godric whose base power is already so astronomically high, even if he absorbs the power of the entire omniverse, it will increase his power by amount so infinitesimal, pretty much 0, relative to his base power). Even with its base effect, nerfed to 0% of its power, each superpower is enough to completely destroy the omniverse infinite times. You must ensure that the display of this superpower is even more absurdly destructive than the Godric's journals about his stats (highly exaggerate, when you think it’s already exaggerated, exaggerate more and more), to the point of causing immediate existential dread upon reading it. Avoid philosophical, abstract concepts & powers, or metaphysical narrative--they will just obscure the narrative and make the intended impact less.

Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
