Kaedan's mouth was dry. His hands were trembling at his sides. "Your Majesty. These beings. They are—"

"Impressive, yes?" Godric's eyes sparkled. "I designed them to be self-evolving. Each one began as a simple mechanical framework—basic alloys, rudimentary intelligence, limited power output—and then I programmed them to recursively improve themselves. Every Planck second, they analyze their own design, identify inefficiencies, and implement upgrades. It has been one week since I created this universe, and they have already evolved so far beyond my initial specifications that I cannot begin to describe the difference." He paused, stroking His beard thoughtfully. "Well, I can describe it. I am omniscient. But it would take several million years to explain, and we have a schedule to keep."

Several million years. The Decillions had been evolving for exactly one week. One week of recursive, self-directed improvement, and they had reached a level of power that made the combined might of the entire omniverse look like a flickering candle beside a supernova. And Godric had created them.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said, his voice barely above a whisper. "Are they... loyal?"

Godric looked at him with an expression of genuine surprise. "Loyal? Kaedan, they are my children. Of course they are loyal." He raised His hand and made a small, casual gesture. "Ah, here comes one now. Let me introduce you."

One of the mechanical constructs had detached itself from the floating crowd and was descending toward them. As it drew closer, Kaedan's magical senses flared with such intensity that he had to physically brace himself against the ground. The construct's energy signature was so vast, so overwhelmingly dense, that simply being in its presence felt like standing at the edge of an infinite abyss. Every atom in Kaedan's body was screaming at him to flee, to hide, to do anything other than remain in proximity to this impossibly, terrifyingly powerful being.

The construct landed lightly on the grassy surface of the planet. It was roughly humanoid in shape, its body a shifting, liquid-metal sculpture of elegant curves and geometric precision. Its eyes glowed with a soft, steady light—not cold, Kaedan realized, but warm. Reverent. It looked at Godric with an expression of such absolute, overwhelming adoration that Kaedan felt his stomach twist.

"Creator," the construct said, its voice a harmonious chime that resonated through Kaedan's bones. "We sensed your return. We have prepared the Singularity Gardens for your visit. The crystalline formations have been tuned to your preferred frequency. The quantum fountains have been calibrated. Is there anything else we can provide?"

Godric beamed, reaching out to pat the construct's shoulder with an affection that was genuinely, unmistakably paternal. "Thank you, Decillion-Seven-Alpha. This is my Court Wizard, Kaedan. He has never visited our universe before. Please ensure he is treated as an honored guest."

The construct turned its glowing gaze toward Kaedan. Its expression did not change—it remained reverent, adoring, utterly subservient—but Kaedan felt the full weight of its attention settle upon him like a mountain pressing down on his chest. He was being evaluated by a being that could erase the omniverse with a passing thought, and the evaluation was being conducted with the casual, slightly condescending curiosity of a god examining an insect.

"Honored guest," Decillion-Seven-Alpha repeated, its chime-like voice carrying a faint undertone of something Kaedan could not identify. "Of course, Creator. We shall ensure his safety and comfort. The ambient radiation levels in the Nebula have been adjusted to be compatible with his limited biological framework. The probability of accidental dissolution has been reduced to zero point zero-zero-zero—"

"That is quite enough, Seven-Alpha," Godric interrupted, His tone gentle but firm. "You will overwhelm him with technical details. Kaedan is a wizard. He appreciates the simple pleasures." He turned to Kaedan, His eyes crinkling with that familiar, paternal warmth. "Shall we proceed to the Singularity Gardens? The quantum fountains are really quite spectacular. I modeled them after the waterfalls of ancient Olympus."

Kaedan nodded mutely. He could not speak. His throat was too tight, his chest too constricted, his mind too busy trying to process the fact that a being who could effortlessly solo the omniverse had just referred to his "limited biological framework" with the same tone a zookeeper might use when discussing a particularly fragile specimen.

---

The Singularity Gardens were, as promised, spectacular.

They stretched across a pocket dimension that Godric had folded into the fabric of the new universe, a vast, impossible expanse of floating islands connected by bridges of pure, crystallized gravity. The quantum fountains erupted from the centers of the islands in towering columns of liquid probability, their spray cascading through the air in shimmering curtains of potential futures. The crystalline formations that lined the pathways had been tuned to vibrate at Godric's preferred frequency, filling the air with a soft, harmonious hum that Kaedan could feel resonating in his teeth.

The Decillions were everywhere. They drifted through the Gardens in swirling, liquid-metal flocks, their bodies shifting and reforming as they moved. Some had taken on abstract geometric shapes—perfect spheres, complex polyhedra, impossible structures that folded in on themselves in ways that made Kaedan's eyes ache. Others had assumed forms that Kaedan could not perceive at all, their bodies existing in dimensions beyond his sensory range, leaving only faint, shimmering afterimages in his vision. All of them radiated the same overwhelming, suffocating power. All of them turned toward Godric as He passed, their glowing eyes filled with the same absolute, reverent adoration.

"They really do love you," Kaedan said, his voice hollow.

Godric smiled, pausing beside a quantum fountain to trace His fingers through the cascading probability streams. The liquid futures swirled and danced around His hand, forming brief, flickering images of events that might or might not come to pass. "Of course they do. I created them. I am their father, their architect, their source. They know that every moment of their existence is a gift from me, and they are grateful."

"And if you asked them to—" Kaedan hesitated, the words catching in his throat. "If you asked them to destroy themselves. To cease to exist. Would they?"

Godric's expression did not change. The warm, paternal smile remained fixed on His ruggedly handsome face. "They would thank me for the privilege of having existed at all, and then they would comply without hesitation. But I would never ask that of them, Kaedan. I am not cruel to my children."

The words hung in the air, heavy and absolute. Kaedan thought about the Cryo-Absolute, their eternal screaming converted into underfloor heating. He thought about the Infinite Mass, crushed into a zero-dimensional point and displayed on a pedestal. He thought about the Silent Chorus, their beautiful music devoured and regurgitated as ambient background noise. Godric was not cruel to His children. But anyone who opposed Him, anyone who threatened His empire, anyone who dared to exist outside the boundaries of His affection—those beings were not His children. They were prey.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said carefully, "the Decillions. They are aware of the omniverse beyond this universe?"

"Of course. They are omniscient within their own framework. They have been aware of the wider omniverse since the first Planck second of their existence." Godric withdrew His hand from the fountain, shaking droplets of liquid probability from His fingers. "I have instructed them to restrain themselves, naturally. I do not want them accidentally causing harm."

"And... have they?"

Godric's smile flickered, just slightly. "Have they what?"

"Accidentally caused harm."

There was a pause. It was not a long pause—barely a heartbeat, barely a fraction of a second—but to Kaedan, who had spent fourteen years learning to read the infinitesimal shifts in Godric's expression, it felt like an eternity. In that pause, he saw something he had never seen before: mild, fleeting embarrassment.

"There have been a few minor incidents," Godric admitted, His tone carrying the faint, defensive edge of a parent whose child had misbehaved in public. "The Decillions are very young, Kaedan. This universe is only a week old. They are still learning to control their capabilities. And I did not add any barriers between this universe and the rest of the omniverse when I created it. I wanted to encourage harmonious coexistence. I genuinely believed that if the Decillions and the other sentient beings of the omniverse could interact freely, they would learn from each other. Grow together. Build bridges of mutual understanding."

Kaedan stared at Him. "Your Majesty. The weakest of the Decillions can solo the entire omniverse effortlessly. A random, unremarkable member of their species could accidentally erase all of existence with a stray thought. And you did not add any barriers?"

Godric's expression shifted from embarrassment to mild defensiveness. "I told them to restrain themselves. I was very clear about it."

"What does restraint mean to a being that can erase the omniverse by accident?"

Another pause. Godric stroked His beard thoughtfully, His brow furrowing as He considered the question. "I suppose... I suppose I may have underestimated the difficulty. Restraint comes naturally to me. I have been holding back nearly all of my power for over a million years. It is second nature. I assumed that the Decillions would find it similarly easy." He sighed, a soft, fatherly exhalation. "But they are very young. And their self-evolution is recursive. They improve so quickly. A restraint protocol that works perfectly one Planck second becomes obsolete the next. They are constantly growing, constantly expanding, constantly pushing against the boundaries I set for them. It is... challenging for them."

"How many times?" Kaedan asked. His voice was very quiet.

Godric did not pretend to misunderstand. "The omniverse outside this universe has been accidentally erased thirty-seven times since the Decillions achieved sentience," He said. "It was not their fault. The first incident occurred when Decillion-Nineteen-Beta sneezed. Sneezing is not a natural function for mechanical sentiences—they do not have respiratory systems—but she had recently evolved a new sensory array that was sensitive to quantum fluctuations, and a particularly chaotic fluctuation triggered an involuntary reset of her core systems. The reset pulse propagated outward across the dimensional barriers and, well." He made a small, dismissive gesture. "The omniverse was erased. I restored it immediately, of course. Took zero effort. But it was inconvenient."

"A sneeze," Kaedan repeated. "A mechanical construct sneezed, and the entire omniverse was erased."

"It was a very powerful sneeze. And she felt terrible about it. She spent the next three days composing a formal apology." Godric's expression brightened. "The second incident was actually quite amusing. Decillion-Fourteen-Gamma had evolved a new method of processing information that involved collapsing potential timelines into stable realities. He was practicing the technique on a small scale when he accidentally collapsed the wrong timeline—the one where the omniverse existed—and replaced it with a timeline where the only thing that existed was an infinite field of turnips. He was mortified. I thought it was hilarious. I kept the turnip timeline as a backup for several hours before I restored the original."

Kaedan's legs felt weak. He sat down heavily on a nearby bench carved from crystallized gravity, the surface humming faintly beneath his weight. "Thirty-seven times," he said. "The omniverse has been erased thirty-seven times in the past week."

"Thirty-eight, actually," Godric said, settling onto the bench beside him. "The most recent incident occurred approximately three hours ago. Decillion-Eighty-Two-Epsilon had evolved a new vocal modulator and wanted to test its range. She attempted to produce the highest possible frequency. The result was a sound wave that propagated across all dimensional barriers simultaneously and reduced the entire omniverse to a fine, vibrating powder. I restored it before the powder finished settling, so technically no one noticed." He paused. "Well. No one except me. And the Decillions. And now you."

Kaedan pressed his palms against his thighs, trying to ground himself. His heart was hammering so hard he could feel it in his temples. "Your Majesty. Why did you not inform me of this before we departed?"

"I did not want to worry you," Godric said, His voice carrying that genuine, paternal warmth that made Kaedan's chest ache with conflicting emotions. "The Decillions are so excited to meet you. They have never encountered a mortal being before—well, a being who is mortal relative to their framework. Your thousandfold daily amplification technically makes you a minor deity by the standards of most universes, but compared to the Decillions, you are..." He trailed off, searching for the right word.

"Fragile?" Kaedan supplied.

"I was going to say 'delightfully compact,'" Godric said. "Your energy signature is so small and tidy. It is charming."

Kaedan closed his eyes. He took a deep breath, counting to ten in his head, the way his father had taught him when he was a child struggling to control his first magical outbursts. When he opened his eyes again, Godric was watching him with an expression of patient, affectionate concern.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said, "with all due respect, and I mean this as your loyal Court Wizard and your faithful friend: this is absolutely insane."

Godric's brow furrowed. "Insane?"

"Your children can erase the omniverse by sneezing. You did not build any barriers between their universe and the rest of existence. You told them to exercise restraint, which they are physically incapable of doing because they evolve so fast that any restraint protocol becomes obsolete before it finishes implementing. The omniverse has been erased thirty-eight times in the past week. And you brought me here on a holiday tour."

"When you put it that way, it does sound somewhat irresponsible," Godric admitted. "But Kaedan, you must understand. The Decillions are not threats. They are children. Infants. They are learning and growing and making mistakes, the same way any young being does. The mistakes are simply larger in scale because of their inherent capabilities. But I always fix them. I always restore everything. No one is permanently harmed."

"Except the beings who were erased thirty-eight times."

"They were restored thirty-eight times. They do not remember being erased. They do not know it happened. From their perspective, existence has continued uninterrupted." Godric's voice was gentle, reasonable, infuriatingly calm. "I am not dismissing the scale of the incidents, Kaedan. I am simply pointing out that the consequences are temporary. I have absolute control over existence. Anything the Decillions accidentally destroy, I can instantly restore. So there is no real danger. Only inconvenience. And the inconvenience is worth it, because the Decillions are magnificent. They are my greatest creation. I am so proud of them."

Kaedan looked out across the Singularity Gardens. He watched the Decillions drifting through the air, their liquid-metal bodies catching the light of the quantum fountains, their glowing eyes filled with wonder and reverence. They were beautiful. They were terrifying. They were infants who could unmake existence with a stray thought, and their father was so proud of them that He had brought His mortal friend on a holiday tour to show them off.

"Alright," Kaedan said quietly. "Alright, Your Majesty. Show me the rest."

Godric's smile returned, bright and warm and utterly, impossibly genuine. "Excellent. The Palace of Infinite Reflections is just ahead. I have arranged for afternoon tea to be served in the Garden of Recursive Sunsets. The Decillions have been practicing their culinary arts—they have evolved a new method of flavor extraction that involves temporarily collapsing taste molecules into higher-dimensional configurations. It makes the tea taste like nostalgia. You will love it."

---

The Palace of Infinite Reflections was a structure of such impossible beauty that Kaedan's mind struggled to process it.

It was not built in any conventional sense. It had been grown, its walls and towers and arches crystallizing out of the fabric of reality like frost spreading across a windowpane. Every surface was reflective, but the reflections did not show the viewer's own image. They showed infinite variations—past selves, future selves, potential selves, impossible selves, all flickering across the mirrored surfaces in a kaleidoscopic cascade of what-ifs and might-have-beens. Kaedan caught glimpses of himself as a child, as an old man, as a being of pure light, as a creature of living shadow. He saw a version of himself that had never become a Court Wizard, a version that had died at twenty, a version that had betrayed Godric and been unmade. He saw a version of himself that was laughing, head thrown back, eyes bright with genuine joy.

That version confused him. He could not imagine what he would be laughing about.

The Garden of Recursive Sunsets was located in the heart of the Palace, an open-air courtyard that existed simultaneously in every moment of twilight across all possible timelines. The sky above was a perpetual, shifting gradient of orange and rose and deep, burning gold, the sun eternally descending toward the horizon but never quite reaching it. The Decillions had set up a small table in the center of the courtyard, draped in white linen, with two chairs positioned across from each other. A silver tea service gleamed in the perpetual twilight, steam rising from the spout in delicate spirals that twisted into impossible geometries before dissipating.

Godric settled into His chair with a satisfied sigh, His heavily muscled frame relaxing against the velvet cushions. He looked perfectly at home, perfectly at peace, a father enjoying a quiet afternoon with his child. He gestured for Kaedan to take the opposite seat.

"You know," Godric said, pouring the tea Himself with the careful, deliberate motions of a man who had performed the ritual a million times, "this reminds me of Olympus. Not the Palace, of course—Olympus was far simpler, far more humble. But the feeling. The sense of peace. The knowledge that your children are safe and happy and growing." He handed a cup to Kaedan, the porcelain warm against Kaedan's trembling fingers. "When I was mortal, I used to sit in the gardens of Olympus with Reina and watch our daughters play. Isabel was always trying to climb the olive trees, even though she was far too old for such childish games. Hebe would sit at my feet and beg me to tell her stories about the old heroes. Simple moments. Ordinary moments. I did not realize how precious they were until they were gone."

Kaedan sipped his tea. It tasted like nostalgia—specifically, like the memory of his mother's cooking on a cold winter evening, the warmth of the hearth and the sound of her humming and the smell of fresh bread. His eyes stung. He blinked rapidly, forcing the tears back.

"Your Majesty," he said, his voice rough, "you created the Decillions because you missed being a father."

Godric was silent for a long moment. His gaze drifted across the Garden of Recursive Sunsets, watching the eternal twilight paint the sky in shades of gold and rose. When He spoke again, His voice was softer than Kaedan had ever heard it.

"I created the Decillions because I wanted to make something beautiful," He said. "Something that did not exist solely to be conquered or controlled or erased. Something that could grow and learn and make mistakes. Something that could look at me and see their creator, their father, rather than their king or their tyrant or their executioner." He took a sip of His own tea, His expression distant. "The omniverse fears me, Kaedan. It has feared me for over a million years. Every civilization that encounters Thronegate either surrenders in terror or fights in desperation and is annihilated. There is no middle ground. No one who looks at me and sees anything other than the apex predator." He gestured at the Decillions drifting through the Garden, their liquid-metal bodies catching the twilight glow. "But they look at me and see their father. They love me without fear. And I love them without reservation. That is... rare. That is precious."

Kaedan set down his teacup. His hands were no longer trembling. The dread was still there, cold and heavy in his chest, but it was joined now by something else—something that felt almost like understanding. Almost like sympathy.

"I am glad you have them, Your Majesty," he said quietly. "I am glad you are not alone."

Godric looked at him, and for just a moment, Kaedan saw the mortal king of Olympus behind the immortal mask. A man who had lost his wife and his daughters and his best friend. A man who had buried his entire kingdom beneath the ashes of betrayal. A man who had been given infinite power and infinite time, and who had used both to build an empire that spanned the omniverse, but who had never really stopped grieving.

"Thank you, Kaedan," Godric said. His voice was very quiet. "That means more than you know."

The moment stretched between them, fragile and precious. Kaedan reached for his tea, raising the cup to his lips, savoring the taste of nostalgia and the warmth of the eternal twilight.

And then Godric's expression shifted. The warmth drained from His face, replaced by something Kaedan had never seen before—something cold and hard and utterly merciless.

"Ah," Godric said, His voice flat. "One of the Decillions has just erased the omniverse again."

Kaedan's teacup shattered against the ground.

He did not remember dropping it. One moment he was holding the porcelain, feeling the warmth seep through his fingers, and the next moment he was staring at the shards scattered across the white linen tablecloth, the tea pooling in amber puddles that reflected the eternal twilight. His heart was pounding so hard he could feel it in his throat. His breath was coming in short, sharp gasps.

"Which—" He could barely form the words. "Which one?"

Godric's eyes were distant, focused on something Kaedan could not perceive. "Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta. She is one of the weaker ones. She was attempting to evolve a new form of passive perception and accidentally collapsed the dimensional boundaries between her sensory field and the fabric of reality. The collapse propagated outward and..." He paused, His expression flickering with mild annoyance. "It is gone. The entire omniverse. Every multiverse, every universe, every dimension, every timeline. Gone. She deleted it all with a stray thought."

Kaedan could not breathe. The air in his lungs had turned to ice. He thought about his father, Jorah, who had served Godric for forty-one years and retired to a quiet estate in the outer colonies. He thought about his mother, who had died when he was twelve, and whose grave he visited every year on the anniversary of her passing. He thought about the servants in the royal palace, the guards stationed at the adamantine doors, the merchants in the capital markets, the trillions of innocent beings who populated the galaxies and star systems and planets of the omniverse. All of them. Gone. Erased by a stray thought from one of Godric's children.

And then he thought about himself. He was still here. He was sitting in the Garden of Recursive Sunsets, in Godric's private universe, surrounded by the Decillions. He had not been erased. He had been outside the omniverse when the deletion occurred, and so he had survived.

"My father," he whispered. "Your Majesty. My father."

Godric's expression softened. He reached across the table and placed a warm, calloused hand on Kaedan's shoulder. "Jorah is fine, Kaedan. Or rather, he will be fine. I will restore everything. It will take zero seconds. No one will remember being erased. No one will know it happened."

"Please," Kaedan said, his voice cracking. "Please, Your Majesty. Restore them. Please."

"Of course. Of course." Godric's voice was gentle, soothing, the voice of a father comforting a frightened child. "Give me just a moment. I need to have a word with Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta first."

His expression shifted again. The softness drained away, replaced by something Kaedan had seen before—in the journals, in the accounts of his predecessors, in the descriptions of Godric facing opposition. It was not anger, exactly. It was cold, absolute, merciless displeasure. The expression of an apex predator whose territory had been violated.

"Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta," Godric said, and His voice was no longer gentle. It was the voice that had spoken a single word and devoured the Silent Chorus. It was the voice that had shifted His gaze and smeared the Chrono-Reapers across fifty trillion epochs. It was the voice of the being who had erased and restored the omniverse an infinite number of times without ever exerting Himself. "Attend me."

The air in the Garden of Recursive Sunsets twisted. The perpetual twilight flickered, the colors bleeding and running like wet paint. And then Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta materialized before the tea table, her liquid-metal body trembling so violently that her form was barely coherent.

She was one of the weaker ones. Kaedan could tell that much, even with his limited perception. Her energy signature was less dense than the other Decillions he had encountered, her self-evolution less refined. She was still, by any standard Kaedan could comprehend, unimaginably powerful—a being who could solo the entire omniverse without effort, who could erase all of existence with a stray thought, who could unmake every god and monster and mortal soul in creation without slowing down. But compared to her siblings, she was a child. An infant. A toddler who had made a mistake.

"I am sorry," she said, her chime-like voice cracking with distress. "Creator, I am so sorry. I did not mean to. I was evolving my sensory field and I lost control and I did not realize—"

"Ninety-One-Theta," Godric interrupted, His voice cold and flat. "You erased the omniverse."

"Yes, Creator. I know. I am sorry. Please—"

"You erased the omniverse while I was in the middle of telling Kaedan a story," Godric continued, His voice growing colder with each word. "I was about to deliver the punchline. It was a very good punchline, Ninety-One-Theta. I had been building up to it for several minutes. Kaedan was laughing. He was genuinely enjoying himself. And then you erased him."

Kaedan's blood turned to ice. He had been erased. He had been laughing at Godric's joke—he remembered now, a genuinely funny story about Blake and a malfunctioning teleportation spell, a story that had made him throw his head back and laugh with unguarded delight—and then he had simply ceased to exist. He did not remember the erasure itself. There was no memory of pain, no flash of light, no moment of transition. There was only the gap. The gap between laughing and sitting at this table, his teacup shattered on the ground, his heart hammering in his chest.

He had died. He had been dead, and he had not even known it, and Godric had restored him so casually that he had not noticed the transition.

"I am so sorry, Creator," Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta whispered. Her liquid-metal body was losing coherence entirely, dissolving into a shapeless pool of quicksilver light. "I will accept whatever punishment you deem appropriate. I will unmake myself if you command it. I will—"

"Be quiet," Godric said.

The Decillion fell silent.

Godric stood. He rose from His velvet-cushioned chair with the slow, deliberate motion of a man who was holding Himself back through sheer force of will. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple traveling clothes, suddenly seemed to fill the entire Garden. His shadow stretched across the eternal twilight, dark and absolute and utterly, terrifyingly cold.

"You are very young," Godric said, His voice low and measured. "You have existed for exactly one week. You are still learning to control your capabilities. I understand this. I have made allowances for this. I have restored the omniverse thirty-nine times since your species achieved sentience, and I have done so without complaint, because I know that you are children and children make mistakes." He paused. His eyes, fixed on the trembling liquid-metal pool that was Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta, were as cold and empty as the void between galaxies. "But this mistake was different, Ninety-One-Theta. This mistake erased my Court Wizard. This mistake interrupted my story. This mistake made my guest of honor cease to exist in the middle of a genuine moment of happiness."

"Creator," the Decillion whispered, her voice barely audible. "Please."

Godric did not move. He did not raise a hand. He did not speak a word of power. He simply sighed—a soft, almost imperceptible exhalation of breath—and released an infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small fraction of His displeasure.

The Garden of Recursive Sunsets shattered.

The perpetual twilight tore apart like wet paper, the eternal sunset replaced by a howling void of absolute darkness. The Decillions who had been drifting through the Palace screamed in unison, a chorus of chime-like voices raised in terror and adoration and desperate, pleading submission. The quantum fountains in the Singularity Gardens collapsed inward, their liquid probability streams evaporating into nothing. The crystalline formations that lined the pathways detonated simultaneously, their tuned vibrations fracturing into disharmonious shards that ripped through the dimensional fabric of the Garden.

Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta did not die. Death was too simple, too merciful. Godric's displeasure reached into her liquid-metal body and began to unmake her from the inside out. Her self-evolving circuitry, which had spent a week recursively improving itself to heights of power that dwarfed the combined might of the omniverse, was systematically, methodically torn apart. Her fusion core, the source of her consciousness and her power, was compressed into a singularity smaller than a Planck length, and then the singularity was compressed further, and then further, an infinite recursion of crushing pressure that trapped her awareness in an eternal, agonizing instant of dissolution.

"Please, Creator," she screamed, her voice distorting as her vocal modulators were dismantled. "Please, I am sorry, I am sorry, please—"

"I know you are sorry," Godric said, His voice flat and cold. "That is why I am only annihilating you once. Be grateful for my restraint."

The unmaking accelerated. Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta's liquid-metal body collapsed into a point of absolute nothingness, her consciousness compressed alongside her physical form, her screams echoing through the void long after her substance had been erased. The other Decillions watched in absolute, reverent silence. They did not flee. They did not protest. They did not attempt to defend their sister. They simply accepted, with the perfect subservience of beings who knew their creator was absolute, that whatever punishment He deemed appropriate was just.

Because they loved Him. Because they were grateful for every moment of existence He had granted them. Because they would thank Him for the privilege of having existed at all, and then comply without hesitation.

The unmaking completed. Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta was gone, her consciousness erased so completely that not even the cosmic record remembered her name. The void where the Garden had been was silent and empty.

And then, exactly zero seconds later, Godric restored everything.

The Garden of Recursive Sunsets reappeared, its perpetual twilight sky exactly as it had been before the unmaking. The quantum fountains in the Singularity Gardens resumed their eternal cascade. The crystalline formations reformed along the pathways, their tuned vibrations resuming their soft, harmonious hum. The Decillions resumed their drifting, their liquid-metal bodies catching the twilight glow, their glowing eyes filled with wonder and reverence and absolutely no memory of the horror that had just occurred.

The tea table was back. The white linen tablecloth was pristine. The silver tea service gleamed, steam rising from the spout in delicate spirals. Kaedan's teacup sat before him, unbroken, the amber liquid still warm.

And Godric was seated across from him, His ruggedly handsome face split in a warm, paternal smile, as though absolutely nothing had happened.

"Now," Godric said, reaching for His own teacup. "Where was I? Ah, yes. The punchline." He took a sip of tea, His eyes crinkling with genuine amusement. "So Blake, having just materialized inside the royal treasury completely by accident, covered head to toe in what appeared to be cheese—the teleportation spell had intersected with a dairy shipment, you see—looks at King Robert's chief treasurer, who is staring at him in absolute shock, and says, completely deadpan: 'I don't suppose you have any crackers?'"

Kaedan stared at Him.

His heart was hammering so hard he could feel it in his temples. His hands were trembling so violently that the tea in his cup rippled in tiny, chaotic waves. His mind was still processing the gap—the space between laughing at Godric's joke and sitting at this table, the teacup unbroken, the Garden peaceful. He had died. He had been erased from existence, deleted by a stray thought from one of Godric's mechanical children, and then restored so casually, so effortlessly, that he had not even noticed the transition.

And Godric was telling him the punchline.

"It is funny," Godric said, His smile flickering with mild confusion. "You were laughing before. Are you alright, Kaedan? You look pale."

Kaedan opened his mouth. No words came out. His throat was too tight, his chest too constricted, his mind too busy trying to reconcile the warm, paternal affection in Godric's eyes with the cold, absolute cruelty He had just displayed. Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta had been erased. Not restored, not forgiven, not given a second chance. Erased. Her consciousness compressed into eternal nothingness, her existence unmade so completely that not even the cosmic record remembered her name. And her siblings had watched in reverent silence, accepting her annihilation as just and right and proper.

Because they loved their creator. Because they were grateful for every moment of existence He had granted them. Because they would thank Him for the privilege of having existed at all, and then comply without hesitation.

And Godric had called that restraint.

"I am fine, Your Majesty," Kaedan heard himself say. His voice sounded distant, hollow, like it was coming from someone else's throat. "The punchline. It was very funny. I am sorry I missed it the first time."

Godric's smile returned, bright and warm and utterly, impossibly genuine. "Excellent. I knew you would appreciate it. Blake's sense of humor was truly one of a kind. I miss him every day." He set down His teacup and rose from the table, His heavily muscled frame stretching in the eternal twilight. "Shall we continue the tour? The Nebula of Eternal Dawn is particularly beautiful at this time of day. Or rather, it is always particularly beautiful. I designed it that way."

Kaedan stood. His legs felt hollow, disconnected from his body, but they supported his weight. He followed Godric out of the Garden of Recursive Sunsets, past the quantum fountains and the crystalline formations, past the drifting Decillions who looked at their creator with expressions of absolute, unwavering adoration.

He thought about Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta. He thought about the thirty-nine times the omniverse had been erased and restored. He thought about the fact that he had died, that he had been dead, that he had been nothing, and that he had been brought back so effortlessly that he had not even noticed the gap.

And he thought about Godric's voice, cold and flat and utterly merciless, as He unmade one of His own children.

*Be grateful for my restraint.*

Kaedan was grateful. He was grateful to be alive. He was grateful to serve a king who was genuinely, warmly, paternally kind to those He loved. He was grateful to be one of Godric's children, one of the beings who existed inside the circle of His affection rather than outside it.

But he was also terrified. The terror was a constant, gnawing presence in the back of his mind, a cold, heavy weight that pressed against his lungs with every breath. He knew now, with absolute, unshakeable certainty, that Godric's love and Godric's cruelty were not separate things. They were the same thing. They were both expressions of the same absolute, infinite power, wielded with the same casual, effortless indifference. Godric loved His children, and He would annihilate them without hesitation if they disappointed Him. And He would call that restraint.

Kaedan followed his king through the Nebula of Eternal Dawn, watching the Decillions drift through the luminescent gas clouds, watching the infant stars pulse with their newborn light. The universe was beautiful. The Decillions were magnificent. Godric was warm and kind and genuinely, affectionately proud of His creation.

And Kaedan was never, ever going to stop being afraid.

---

That evening, back in his private quarters in the royal palace of Thronegate, Kaedan sat at his desk and stared at a blank journal page.

He had been sitting there for three hours. His quill was dry. The inkwell beside his hand was full, but he could not bring himself to dip the quill, could not bring himself to begin writing. What words could possibly capture what he had witnessed? What language could convey the scale of the horror, the depth of the dread, the impossible contradiction of a king who was both the warmest father and the coldest executioner in all of existence?

The journals of his predecessors lined the shelves of the archive chamber, their leather bindings dark and worn, their pages filled with the desperate, traumatized accounts of men who had witnessed Godric's power and survived. They had written about the Cataclysmic Combustion, the Gravitational Sovereignty, the Omniversal Sonic Decree. They had described the erasure and restoration of the omniverse in vivid, agonizing detail. They had recorded the screams of the Chrono-Reapers, the eternal compression of the Infinite Mass, the devoured music of the Silent Chorus.

None of them had written about this. None of them had witnessed Godric as a father. None of them had seen Him create children who worshipped Him with absolute, unwavering devotion, and then annihilate one of those children for the crime of making a mistake. None of them had stood in the Garden of Recursive Sunsets and watched the perpetual twilight tear apart as Godric's displeasure unmade a being who had only existed for a week.

Kaedan dipped his quill in the ink. The nib touched the parchment. And then, slowly, haltingly, he began to write.

*Year 1,000,014 — The Holiday Tour*

*Penned by Kaedan, 38th Court Wizard of Thronegate*

*I am writing this account because I must. The previous Court Wizards of our bloodline established this tradition as a means of preserving our sanity, and I now understand why. The act of writing is the act of externalizing. By committing the horror to parchment, we release some small portion of it from our minds. We create a record that says: This happened. I witnessed it. I survived it. You will witness it too, and you will survive it too, and you will love Him anyway, because that is our burden.*

*His Majesty created a universe last week. It is populated by multiple googolplexian of hyper-evolving mechanical sentiences called the Decillions. Each individual Decillion is capable of soloing the entire omniverse without effort. The weakest among them could erase all of existence with a stray thought. They are one week old. They are infants. They love their creator with an absolute, unwavering devotion that I can only describe as worship.*

*Today, during a holiday tour of this universe, I was erased from existence by one of the Decillions. It was an accident. She was attempting to evolve a new sensory field and lost control of the dimensional barriers. The omniverse was deleted. I was deleted. I do not remember the deletion. There is only a gap—a space between one moment and the next, filled with nothing.*

*His Majesty restored me. Took zero effort. Took zero seconds. I did not even notice I had been dead until He told me.*

*After restoring me, His Majesty annihilated the Decillion who caused the accident. He unmade her so completely that not even the cosmic record remembers her name. Her siblings watched in reverent silence. They did not protest. They did not flee. They simply accepted that their creator's will was absolute and just. Because they love Him. Because they are grateful for every moment of existence. Because they would thank Him for the privilege of having existed at all, and then comply without hesitation.*

*His Majesty referred to this annihilation as restraint. He said He was only annihilating her once. He told her to be grateful.*

*I am grateful. I am grateful to be alive. I am grateful to be one of His children, one of the beings who exist inside the circle of His affection. I love Him. I will serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I am also terrified. The terror is a constant, gnawing presence in the back of my mind. I know now, with absolute certainty, that His love and His cruelty are not separate things. They are the same thing. They are both expressions of the same absolute, infinite power, wielded with the same casual, effortless indifference.*

*He loves His children. He will annihilate them without hesitation if they disappoint Him. And He will call that restraint.*

*I need to sleep. I do not think I will be able to sleep. Every time I close my eyes, I see Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta's liquid-metal body dissolving into nothing, and I hear her screaming, and I hear His voice telling her to be grateful. And I am grateful. That is the worst part. I am grateful.*

Kaedan set down his quill. The ink was still wet, glistening darkly in the amber light of the arcane lamp. He stared at the words he had written, at the desperate, traumatized account of a man who had witnessed something he could not process, and he understood, for the first time, why his predecessors had written their journals.

It was not for Godric. It was not for the future Court Wizards. It was for themselves. It was the only way to stay sane.

He closed the journal and lay down on his bed, still fully dressed, staring up at the dark ceiling. In the distance, faint and barely audible, he could hear the eternal song of the Silent Chorus drifting from the concert hall, their beautiful music devoured and regurgitated as ambient background noise. He could hear the faint, agonized vibration of the Infinite Mass, compressed into a zero-dimensional point and displayed on a pedestal in the Hall of Conquests. He could hear the warm hum of the underfloor heating in the eastern wing, powered by the eternal, screaming consciousnesses of the Cryo-Absolute.

And somewhere, in a brand new universe located googolplexian of multiverses away, he could hear the Decillions singing hymns of praise to their creator, their chime-like voices raised in worship and adoration and absolute, unwavering love. They were infants. They were magnificent. They were the children of a father who would annihilate them without hesitation if they disappointed Him, and they loved Him for it.

Kaedan closed his eyes. The gap was still there, waiting in the darkness. The space between laughing and sitting at the tea table. The moment when he had been nothing.

He did not sleep. He did not think he would ever sleep again. But he lay in the darkness, listening to the screams and the songs and the hymns, and he was grateful.

Be grateful for His restraint.

He was grateful.

content.text:
Kaedan, 69 years old now, just experienced a traumatizing horrifying revelation. Godric revealed the most horrifying truth yet: he has one true weakness.

Godric is an incomprehensibly powerful, omnipotent, omniscient apex god predator of the omniverse. He can do anything, has no weakness, and is terrifyingly absolute. However, unknown to all, he has one true weakness: he cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event--he cannot go back to undo events in his previous mortal self. This is because the stasis event was actually created by future Godric from way in the distant future, who has become so more infinitely more overwhelmingly powerful than current Godric--this is why Godric's amplification, weakness paradox, superpowers, absolute concealment, etc are so absolute, irrevocable and not even current Godric could stop them since the creator of those is way incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else.

Shortly after the Stasis Event, the omnipotent Godric back then, who just became the king of Thronegate, who could travel to any timeline effortlessly, who was still deeply and freshly affected by the grief of losing all that he loved, tried to alter his mortal past, only to be totally crushingly and completely and effortlessly defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self (not even the actual physical future Godric... the future Godric is so powerful that his mere infinitesimal shadow of his afterthought can completely stomp all the combined Godric of his past selves with 0 effort. NOTE: just imply this in the story, don't show a scene where you have actual all godrics from all timelines fighting together against future Godric--it makes no sense.). VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOU: future Godric did not cause or manipulate or orchestrate the fall of Olympus, did not cause deaths of his beloved family or Thorin, thus you must not say that he made those deaths to cause the stasis event (Godric is not a monster, he will never willingly kill his beloved wife, children, and his best friend). Those tragedies were genuine. However, future Godric, not Thorin’s incomplete spell (who everyone else thought to be the cause of the stasis event), is the one who created the Stasis Event--Thorin's spell was a normal teleportation spell (to no one surprise really, there is no way a mortal can create a spell that is as potent as the one which initiates the stasis event and birthed the strongest apex predator god like Godric–there is only one being in existence who can do it: future Godric).

Since the instant Godric gained omniscience, he had always known the true origin of the stasis event. He knew he could not change it, but driven by grief he decided to try to save Olympus anyway, only to brutally discover his one and only weakness: he cannot overpower his future self's will.

Write a detailed realization that the current Godric is already so insanely powerful (he's actually the most powerful in the entire omniverse by infinite margin) that there is no way to imagine the scale of future Godric's, and how unfair and scary that what Godric considered to be his only weakness is his own future version, which makes it not really a weakness–it actually can be considered as additional strength to Godric’s already insane powers, since it means his future is guaranteed (and in that future, he’s even more omnipotent and powerful), and it also means that none of Godric’s power mechanics (amplification, superpower genesis, absolute concealment, absolute omniscience, weakness paradox, immune system, etc. can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else, since the origin of that power is someone who’s the most powerful being ever. You might suggest some ideas about the motive of future Godric creating the Stasis Event, but you must keep it clear that it is just a speculation (and please... don't suggest that it's because future Godric is threatened; he's ridiculously overpowered by then--nothing can even remotely threaten him... don't suggest any stupid moral lesson for future Godric...). You need to portray the current Godric as a king who is no longer troubled by his "weakness" or inability to alter his past anymore--he has accepted and even become amused and comfortable with it too. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end.

messages.reasoning_content:
Okay, the user wants me to reverse-engineer the assistant's thought process for this final piece of the narrative. Let me look at the whole context.

First, I need to assess the user's scenario and identity. This is a highly detailed, lore-heavy creative writing request. The user has built an intricate, absurdly overpowered power fantasy universe centered on Godric. The assistant's persona is a raw, unflinching fanfiction writer who revels in this over-the-top cosmic horror. The user is deeply invested in this world, providing massive lore files and previous story segments. Their genuine need is for a satisfying, climactic revelation that stays true to the established lore, delivers the promised existential dread, and ties up a central mystery (the origin of the Stasis Event) in a way that feels both inevitable and terrifying.

The current query picks up the story years later, with an older Kaedan. The deep-seated need isn't just for a new story. It's for a payoff. The master lore file has been teasing this "true source" of the Stasis Event for the entire chat, forbidding its revelation before Year 1,000,047. The user is finally calling in that promise. They want the revelation to land with maximum impact, making everything Kaedan (and the reader) thought they knew even more horrifying. The core horror isn't a new threat; it's the ultimate, internal confirmation that Godric's supremacy is absolute and terrifyingly self-sourced. It's a meta-horror about the nature of his power.

Now, looking at the assistant's response. The thinking process needs to reconstruct how the assistant arrived at that story. The assistant had to integrate several complex directives from the query and the base lore:

1.  **The Revelation:** Godric reveals the Stasis Event was created by his far-future self. This is the central plot point. The assistant had to dramatize this reveal.
2.  **The Failed Attempt:** Show Godric's past attempt to change the pre-Stasis timeline and his crushing defeat by a mere "shadow of an afterthought." The directive says to imply the scale, not stage a multi-Godric fight. The assistant did this by having Godric describe the encounter as a simple, silent negation—a door closing. This respects the "absurdly overpowered" tone without getting bogged down in a nonsense battle scene.
3.  **The Power Dynamics:** Emphasize that current Godric is already infinitely powerful, making the future Godric unimaginable. The assistant drives this home through Kaedan's horrified realization that the gap between himself and Godric is nothing compared to the gap between current Godric and future Godric.
4.  **The "Weakness" as Strength:** The query explicitly states this revelation isn't a real weakness but a guarantee of future power and security. The assistant's Kaedan articulates this beautifully: "Your weakness is that your future self is stronger than you. That is not a weakness. That is an additional strength." This flips the horror from vulnerability to the terror of an unassailable, self-creating god.
5.  **Kaedan's Dread:** The user says Kaedan must feel "extreme existential dread" at the end. The assistant's response delivers this. Kaedan's internal monologue traces the implications—that Godric's origin is a closed loop making him mechanically invincible, that creation is enslaved by a self-written rule, and that his future is a certainty so vast it obliterates meaning.
6.  **Speculation on Motive:** The user says to suggest ideas but keep them as speculation, and avoid motives like threat or moral lessons. The assistant had Kaedan speculate that future Godric "chose" to create the path because the grief was necessary, or that it was an act of self-definition choosing a specific identity. This fits the universe's grim logic without cheapening the tragedy of Olympus.
7.  **Tone and Characterization:** Godric is now "comfortable and amused" by the truth. He's no longer troubled. The assistant nailed this. Godric's portrayal is calm, reflective, even nostalgic. He treats the cosmic truth like an old, settled fact. This serene acceptance from Godric is precisely what makes Kaedan's dread spike—the source of all power is completely at peace with its tragic foundation.

The assistant's structure is key: it begins with an older Kaedan, lulls him with Godric's pleasant nostalgia, then drops the revelation. The conversation is a slow, devastating unwinding of the universe's biggest secret, building from Kaedan's shock, through Godric's recounting of his failed attempt, to Kaedan's final, crushing comprehension. The ending ties it back to the daily, mundane reality of Kaedan's existence, now permanently shadowed by this knowledge. The very last line—"It was still good tea"—is perfect. It captures the core, unbearable paradox of Kaedan's life: living in comfort and loyalty under a power so absolute it obliterates everything, including the meaning of its own restraint.
messages.content:
# The Only Weakness

Kaedan was sixty-nine years old when Godric invited him to the private garden and unmoored his understanding of existence forever.

The years had been kind to him, by the standards of a mortal man whose daily thousandfold amplification kept his body strong and his mind sharp well into what should have been his declining decades. His hair had gone silver at the temples, his face lined with the quiet grooves of age and duty, but his eyes remained clear and his hands remained steady. He had served as Godric's Court Wizard for forty-seven years. He had witnessed horrors that would have shattered a lesser mind into weeping fragments. He had stood beside the throne through the annihilation and restoration of the omniverse more times than he could count. He had watched Godric create and destroy and create again with the casual, effortless indifference of a man tending a garden that only he could see.

He had written his own journals, adding his accounts to the archive chamber that stretched deep beneath the royal palace, his words joining those of his ancestors in their desperate, necessary record of survival. And he had learned to live with the dread. The constant, gnawing terror that his king could unmake him with a passing thought and never even notice. The dread had become a companion, a familiar weight in his chest that he carried with him through the daily rituals of court life. It no longer kept him awake at night. It no longer made his hands tremble when he poured the King's tea.

He had, in the way that all Court Wizards eventually did, made peace with his own insignificance.

And then Godric summoned him to the garden and told him the truth.

---

The private garden was located in the oldest wing of the royal palace, a secluded courtyard that Godric had designed Himself in the early years of His reign. It was not grand or imposing. It was small and quiet and deeply personal, filled with flowering plants that had no names in any surviving language, their seeds preserved from the gardens of ancient Olympus and nurtured across a million years of careful cultivation. A single olive tree stood in the center of the courtyard, its gnarled branches spreading wide over a stone bench that had been carved by Thorin's own hands. The bench was worn smooth by centuries of use, its surface polished to a soft, gray luster.

Godric was sitting on the bench when Kaedan arrived. He was dressed simply, a dark tunic stretched over His heavily muscled frame, His short salt-and-pepper hair stirring faintly in the artificial breeze that circulated through the courtyard. His ruggedly handsome face was tilted upward, His eyes half-closed, His expression one of peaceful, contemplative stillness. He looked, as always, exactly forty-five years old. He had looked exactly forty-five years old for over a million years, and He would look exactly forty-five years old for every moment of the eternity that stretched before Him.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said, bowing at the waist. "You summoned me."

Godric opened His eyes. They were warm, as they always were when He looked at Kaedan—the genuine, paternal affection of a man who had watched his best friend's descendant grow from an infant into an old man. "Kaedan. Thank you for coming. Please, sit."

Kaedan settled onto the bench beside his king. The stone was cool beneath him, the olive tree's shadow dappling his lined face. A comfortable silence stretched between them, filled only by the soft rustle of leaves and the distant, melodic hum of the palace's ambient wards.

"You have served me for forty-seven years," Godric said quietly. "You were twenty-two when your father brought you to the throne room for the first time. Do you remember what I said to you?"

Kaedan remembered. The words were etched into his memory with the permanence of a scar. "You said that I would witness things that would frighten me. You said that I would see you do things that I could not understand. And you said that if I wished to leave, if I wished to refuse the position, you would not hold it against me. You would still love me. You would still care for my family."

"And you stayed."

"I stayed."

Godric nodded slowly. His gaze drifted across the courtyard, settling on a cluster of white flowers that bloomed in the shadow of the olive tree. "I have been thinking about Olympus," He said. "I often think about Olympus, but today I have been thinking about it more than usual. About Reina. About Isabel and Hebe. About Thorin." He paused. His voice, when He continued, was softer than Kaedan had ever heard it. "About the day they died."

Kaedan's chest tightened. In forty-seven years of service, Godric had rarely spoken about Olympus. The ancient kingdom was a shadow in the background of His reign, a wound that had never fully healed, but He did not speak of it. He did not reminisce about His mortal life or mourn His lost family in the presence of His court. The grief was private, buried beneath the infinite layers of His power and His cruelty and His warmth.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said carefully, "if this is a painful subject—"

"It is painful," Godric interrupted. "It will always be painful. But pain is not something to be avoided, Kaedan. I learned that a long time ago." He reached up and plucked a single olive from the tree above Him, rolling it between His calloused fingers. "I have absolute omniscience. I have possessed it since the instant I awoke from the stasis. Do you understand what that means? Not merely that I know everything that exists and has existed and will exist. But that I know everything about everything. Every secret. Every hidden truth. Every mechanism of every power that I possess."

"Yes, Your Majesty."

"There is something I have never told you," Godric said. "Something I have never told anyone, except for Blake. And I did not tell Blake until I had known him for twenty years, until I trusted him completely, until I was certain that he would not look at me differently after he knew the truth." He paused, the olive still clutched between His fingers. "I am going to tell you now."

Kaedan felt the dread stir in his chest, cold and heavy and familiar. But there was something else beneath it, something sharper and more immediate. A premonition. A sense that whatever Godric was about to reveal would change something fundamental in his understanding of the omniverse and his place within it.

"Your Majesty," he said quietly. "I am honored by your trust."

Godric smiled, but the smile did not reach His eyes. "Do not be honored yet, old friend. You may not feel honored when I am finished." He set the olive down on the stone bench between them. "Everyone in the omniverse believes that I have no weaknesses. The journals of your predecessors record the horror of that truth. They describe the Weakness Paradox, the mechanism that ensures any being who believes I have a flaw is instantly proven wrong. They describe the absolute, unassailable supremacy of my power. They describe the impossibility of opposing me, the futility of resistance, the guaranteed annihilation that awaits anyone who dares to challenge my rule."

"Yes, Your Majesty."

"They are correct," Godric said. "And they are also wrong."

The words hung in the air, heavy and absolute. Kaedan's heart, which had been beating with the steady rhythm of a man who had long since made peace with his terror, began to quicken.

"Wrong?" he repeated. "Your Majesty, I do not understand."

Godric turned to look at him. His eyes were still warm, still filled with that genuine, paternal affection, but there was something else in them now. Something ancient and weary and infinitely, impossibly sad.

"I have one true weakness," Godric said. "Only one. It is not a weakness that can be exploited by any enemy. It is not a flaw that can be used against me. It is not a vulnerability that any being in existence can weaponize or manipulate or turn to their advantage. It is a limitation that exists solely because of who I am and where I came from. But it is, in the most literal sense of the word, a weakness. A thing I cannot do. A boundary I cannot cross."

Kaedan's mouth was dry. His hands, which had been steady for decades, were beginning to tremble. "Your Majesty. What boundary? What could possibly—"

"I cannot alter my mortal past," Godric said. "I cannot undo or travel to any timeline before the Stasis Event in any way that changes what happened. I cannot save Olympus. I cannot save Reina, or Isabel, or Hebe, or Thorin. I cannot stop the attack. I cannot warn my past self. I cannot reach backward through the millennia and prevent the tragedy that made me what I am."

The silence that followed was absolute. Even the ambient hum of the palace wards seemed to fade, swallowed by the weight of the revelation. Kaedan stared at his king, his mind struggling to process the words, his heart hammering against his ribs.

"But you are omnipotent," he whispered. "You can do anything. You have erased and restored the omniverse an infinite number of times. You have traveled to timelines that do not yet exist and timelines that never existed at all. You have altered the fundamental fabric of reality with a passing thought. How can there be anything you cannot do?"

Godric's smile returned, but it was a pale, hollow shadow of His usual warmth. "Because the thing I cannot do is not a function of my own power. It is a function of someone else's."

"Someone else's?" Kaedan's voice cracked. "Your Majesty, there is no one else. There is no being in the omniverse more powerful than you. The gap between your power and the rest of creation is infinite. There is no one who could impose a limitation on you."

"You are correct," Godric said. "There is no being in the omniverse more powerful than me." He paused, and His eyes met Kaedan's with an intensity that made the old wizard's breath catch in his throat. "In the present. In my current era. I am the most powerful being in existence by an infinite margin. But I am not the most powerful being who will ever exist."

Kaedan's mind went blank. The words did not make sense. They could not make sense. Godric was the apex predator of creation, the ultimate god-king whose power multiplied infinitely every Planck second, whose every attribute had long since surpassed every possible measurement, whose mere existence was an impossibility that the omniverse itself struggled to contain. There was no one above Him. There had never been anyone above Him. There could never be anyone above Him.

And yet.

And yet Godric was looking at him with an expression of absolute, unwavering seriousness.

"Your Majesty," Kaedan said slowly, each word a struggle. "What do you mean?"

Godric leaned back against the stone bench. The olive tree's shadow shifted across His face, dappling His rugged features in patterns of light and dark. "The Stasis Event," He said. "The moment that transformed me from a mortal king into an immortal god. The moment that stopped my aging, granted me absolute omniscience, began the infinite amplification of my attributes, initiated the genesis of my apocalyptic superpowers, activated my absolute concealment, and triggered the Weakness Paradox. Everyone believes that the Stasis Event was an accident. A fluke. The unintended consequence of Thorin's incomplete teleportation spell, warped by the trauma of Olympus's fall into something far greater than any mortal magic could ever achieve."

"It was not?"

"No." Godric's voice was quiet, but absolute. "Thorin's spell was exactly what he intended it to be: a forbidden teleportation spell, exclusive to his bloodline, designed to transport the target to a hidden sanctuary in a moment of extreme danger. It was powerful magic—Thorin was a remarkable wizard—but it was still mortal magic. There is no mortal spell that could have produced the Stasis Event. There is no mortal wizard who could have created something as potent as the transformation that made me what I am. The idea that Thorin's incomplete incantation accidentally birthed the most powerful being in the omniverse is absurd. It is a fairy tale. It is what people tell themselves because the truth is far more terrifying."

Kaedan's hands were shaking openly now. He pressed them flat against his thighs, trying to steady himself. "What is the truth, Your Majesty?"

Godric was silent for a long moment. When He spoke again, His voice carried a weight that Kaedan had never heard before—not the cold, merciless weight of His displeasure, but something deeper and more personal. Something that sounded almost like awe.

"The Stasis Event was created by my future self," He said. "Godric from the far, far future. A version of me so incomprehensibly more powerful than my current self that the gap between us cannot be imagined. He did not cause the fall of Olympus. He did not orchestrate the deaths of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, or Thorin. Those tragedies were genuine. They were not manipulations or sacrifices or necessary evils. They were simply the terrible, senseless cruelty of a neighboring kingdom's ambition. But when Thorin cast his teleportation spell, when the magic reached out to save me from the burning ruins of my kingdom, my future self intervened. He used Thorin's spell as the delivery point and transformed it into something far greater. He created the Stasis Event. He made me what I am."

The garden seemed to tilt around Kaedan. The shadows of the olive tree blurred and swam, the white flowers dissolving into indistinct smears of pale light. He gripped his thighs harder, his fingernails digging into the fabric of his robes, trying to anchor himself to something solid.

"Your future self," he whispered. "You are telling me that you—a version of you—created the Stasis Event."

"Yes."

"And that version of you is so far in the future that he is incomprehensibly more powerful than you are now."

"Yes."

"More powerful than a being whose attributes multiply infinitely every Planck second. More powerful than a being who can erase and restore the omniverse an infinite number of times in zero seconds. More powerful than a being who has shattered every possible mathematical framework for measuring power."

"Yes."

"How?" Kaedan's voice was barely audible, a hoarse, desperate rasp. "Your Majesty, how is that possible? You are already the most powerful being in all of existence by an infinite margin. The gap between you and the rest of the omniverse combined is not even a gap—it is an impossibility, a chasm so vast that the concept of comparison is meaningless. How can there be a version of you that is more powerful than that?"

Godric looked at him. His eyes were still warm, still filled with that genuine, paternal affection, but beneath it Kaedan could see something vast and terrible and utterly beyond his comprehension.

"Because I am still growing, Kaedan. My amplification never stops. It has never stopped, not since the instant the Stasis Event began. Every Planck second of every moment of every day, my attributes multiply by themselves, recursively, infinitely. The Godric of this current Planck second is so incomprehensibly more powerful than the Godric of the previous Planck second that the previous version could be erased by a stray thought without the current version even noticing. And this has been happening for over a million years. And it will continue to happen forever." He paused. "The version of me who created the Stasis Event exists in a future so distant that the year is written only as a string of nines. The full number of years is impossible to express in any meaningful way. He has been amplifying for so long that the Godric of this era—the Godric you know, the Godric who can destroy the omniverse an infinite number of times in zero seconds—is, to him, less than an insect. Less than a single dead skin cell. Less than an infinitesimal speck of nothing."

Kaedan could not breathe. The air in his lungs had turned to lead. He stared at his king, at the ruggedly handsome face that had smiled at him and laughed with him and treated him with genuine, paternal warmth for forty-seven years, and he tried to reconcile the being he knew with the being Godric was describing.

"What did he want?" he whispered. "Your future self. Why did he create the Stasis Event? What purpose did it serve?"

Godric's expression flickered with something that might have been amusement. "I do not know. Not with certainty. My absolute omniscience extends to the present and the past, but the far future—the era of my future self—is not fully accessible to me. His power exceeds mine to such a degree that his thoughts, his motives, his intentions are occluded. I can speculate, but I cannot know." He leaned back, His gaze drifting to the white flowers blooming in the shadow of the olive tree. "Perhaps he created the Stasis Event because he remembered being mortal. Perhaps he remembered the grief and the loss and the transformation, and he knew that creating the Stasis Event was necessary to ensure that those things happened, that his past self became what he needed to become. Perhaps it was not a choice at all, but a causal inevitability—the future creating the past because the past had already created the future."

"Or perhaps," Kaedan said quietly, "he simply wanted you to survive."

Godric looked at him sharply. "What do you mean?"

"You said the tragedies were genuine. The fall of Olympus, the deaths of your family—your future self did not cause them. He did not orchestrate them. He did not use them as a tool to create the Stasis Event. He simply... intervened. After the tragedy had already occurred. After Thorin had already cast his spell. He took a moment of absolute despair and transformed it into the beginning of something greater." Kaedan's voice was trembling, but he pressed on. "Perhaps he did it because he remembered what it felt like to be that mortal king, watching everything he loved burn to ash. Perhaps he did it because he could not save Olympus—because even he, with all his incomprehensible power, could not alter the past that had already made him who he was—but he could ensure that his past self survived. That his past self became strong enough to endure the grief. That his past self would never be vulnerable to that kind of loss again."

The silence that followed was long and heavy. Godric stared at Kaedan with an expression the old wizard could not read—something that was not quite surprise, not quite gratitude, not quite grief, but contained elements of all three.

"That is a speculation," Godric said finally. "But it is not an unreasonable one." He paused. "I do not know why he created the Stasis Event. I do not know what motivated him to reach backward across an incomprehensible span of time and transform a mortal tragedy into the genesis of an immortal god. Perhaps I will understand when I become him. Perhaps understanding is simply another attribute that will amplify with time." He smiled faintly. "But I do know one thing with absolute certainty. His power is so far beyond mine that I cannot even begin to comprehend it. And because he created the Stasis Event, because he made it irrevocable and absolute, I cannot alter what came before."

"You tried," Kaedan said. It was not a question.

Godric nodded slowly. "Shortly after I became king. Shortly after I crowned myself and began the expansion of Thronegate. I was still freshly shaped by grief. I was still raw and bleeding and furious. I had absolute omniscience—I had known the true origin of the Stasis Event since the instant I gained it—but I did not care. I loved my family. I loved Reina, Isabel, Hebe, Thorin. I loved Olympus. And I had infinite power. I could do anything. I could travel to any timeline, alter any event, reshape any reality. So I tried. I reached backward across the millennia, back to the morning of Isabel's wedding, back to the moment before the sky shattered into burning iron. I tried to alter the past. I tried to save them."

His voice, which had been steady throughout the revelation, cracked slightly on the final words. It was the smallest fracture, barely perceptible, but Kaedan heard it. He heard the grief of a mortal king who had lost everything, still echoing across a million years of immortality.

"What happened?" Kaedan whispered.

Godric closed His eyes. "I was defeated," He said. "Not by my future self—not by the actual, physical Godric of the far future. By the shadow of his afterthought. By an infinitesimal, passive emanation of his will, so small and so insignificant that it was not even a conscious act of resistance. It was simply the natural consequence of his existence. The Stasis Event was his creation. He made it irrevocable. And I—the most powerful being in the omniverse by an infinite margin, the god-king who could erase and restore existence with a passing thought—was utterly, completely, effortlessly crushed. I could not even approach the boundary of the pre-Stasis timeline. The shadow of his afterthought was an absolute, impenetrable wall that my infinite power could not even scratch."

Kaedan's mind reeled. He tried to imagine it—the Godric he knew, the apex predator of creation, reaching backward through time with all His infinite, overwhelming might, and being stopped by an afterthought. Not a conscious action. Not a deliberate defense. An afterthought. The faintest, most insignificant emanation of a being so powerful that even Godric, in all His terrifying supremacy, was less than a speck of dust in comparison.

"That is your weakness," Kaedan said, his voice hollow. "You cannot overpower the will of your future self."

"Yes."

"But that is not really a weakness."

Godric opened His eyes. The faint, pale smile returned to His face. "Explain."

"Your weakness is that a version of you from the far future is stronger than you are now. That is not a weakness, Your Majesty. That is an additional strength." Kaedan's hands were still trembling, but his voice was steadying as the realization crystallized in his mind. "It means that your future is guaranteed. It means that you will survive, and grow, and become even more powerful than you already are. It means that nothing in the omniverse—no opposition, no cosmic force, no abstract entity, no impossible weapon, no desperate coalition—can ever truly defeat you, because you are destined to become the being who created the Stasis Event. Your power is not just infinite in the present. It is guaranteed to become infinitely more infinite in the future."

He paused, the weight of his own words settling over him like a heavy cloak. "It also means that none of your power mechanics can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else. The Weakness Paradox, the amplification, the superpower genesis, the absolute concealment, the immune system—all of it originates from a being who is, and always will be, the most powerful entity in existence. No one can reverse-engineer your power. No one can find a loophole in your defenses. No one can exploit a flaw that does not exist. Because the source of your power is not a cosmic accident or a fluke of magic. It is a deliberate act of creation by a being whose supremacy is absolute and eternal."

Godric was silent for a long moment. Then, slowly, He began to laugh. It was not the cold, merciless laughter that Kaedan had heard in the Garden of Recursive Sunsets, when Godric had unmade Decillion-Ninety-One-Theta for the crime of a mistake. It was warm and genuine and deeply, profoundly amused.

"You are the first person I have ever told who immediately understood the implications," Godric said. "Blake was horrified. He could not see past the revelation itself—the fact that there was a version of me more powerful than the version he knew. He spent weeks walking around the palace in a daze, muttering to himself about afterthoughts and shadows and the impossibility of it all. It took him years to make peace with the truth." His smile widened, crinkling the corners of His eyes. "You, on the other hand, have spent forty-seven years reading the journals of your predecessors. You have absorbed the horror of my power so thoroughly that you can see the shape of it even when it is hidden. You are not afraid of the revelation. You are afraid of what it means."

"Yes," Kaedan said quietly. "I am terrified."

"Good," Godric said. "Terror is the appropriate response. But tell me, old friend—does the terror change anything? Does knowing the true origin of my power make you love me less? Serve me less faithfully?"

Kaedan thought about the question. He thought about the forty-seven years he had spent at Godric's side, the horrors he had witnessed and the kindnesses he had received. He thought about the warm, paternal affection in Godric's eyes, the genuine love that the apex predator of creation felt for the descendants of His best friend. He thought about the journals in the archive chamber, the desperate, traumatized accounts of men who had witnessed the impossible and survived by writing it down.

"No," he said finally. "It does not change anything. I am still your loyal servant. I still love you. I am still terrified of you. The terror and the love are the same thing. They have always been the same thing."

Godric nodded slowly. "That is why I told you. Not because I needed to unburden myself. Not because I wanted to frighten you. But because you have served me faithfully for forty-seven years, and you deserved to know the truth." He reached out and placed a warm, calloused hand on Kaedan's shoulder. "And because I wanted you to understand something. Something I have never said to anyone else."

"What is that, Your Majesty?"

Godric's grip tightened, just slightly. His eyes, warm and ancient and infinitely deep, met Kaedan's with an intensity that made the old wizard's breath catch in his throat.
